trajectory_summary: The call opens with a self-referred, inbound customer who has real interest but arrives with a constraint stack (no front-roof panels, new roof anxiety, prior Sunrun bad experience). Lisa self-generates the appointment ask at 06:11, which is a genuine positive signal. However, the booking is structurally fragile: Lisa explicitly frames the appointment as a chance to verify a billing claim she doesn't yet believe, the rep made a partial-offset concession that deepened rather than resolved her skepticism, and the button-up is missing four of six required elements. The call ends booked but with the trust gap unrepaired.
outcome_dispute: The call is booked, but the booking quality is conditional. Lisa's stated reason for agreeing to the appointment is to 'understand further your philosophy and where that bill is going to be vaporized from the electric company totally' — she is coming to verify a claim she doubts, not to evaluate a solution she's excited about. The closer should treat this as a fragile sit, not a warm one.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Escape hatch deployment — when to stop educating and redirect to the appointment
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Sometimes we're not able to offset it a 100%. Sometimes we're only able to cover, you know, half of the usage.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Lisa drove the billing education exchange, but the rep's partial-offset concession at this line is where the call's trust trajectory reversed. The rep had the escape hatch available ('that's exactly what the specialist calculates — I can't give you a number until we see your design') and didn't use it. This is the single move that, if made one exchange earlier, changes the appointment's hold probability. The Sunrun reframe opportunity at 05:18 is the companion moment — both belong in the same coaching conversation about when to stop defending and start redirecting.

key_moments.quote: Hi. This is Joe with Ion. Joe Joe, hi. This is Lisa Chris Smith. Kevin called and left me a message.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep used no Ion intro template — no Tesla framing, no form-fill reference, no state mention, no 'how are you doing' pause. The call opens as a callback on a garbled number, which creates lead-origin ambiguity (schema lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules requires lead type to interpret bill-collection effort correctly). The rep never establishes which intro variant applies, which means legitimacy is not anchored in the first 30 seconds as the schema requires.

key_moments.quote: Well, what would if I had a shed and wanted to put solar on a shed, would that suffice?
key_moments.stewart_read: Lisa's shed question is a secondary-structure solar signal. The rep dismissed it on structural grounds ('the panels would be a bit too heavy for that') without routing to a specialist or flagging it as a potential cross-sell. The schema's cross_sell_signals section marks solar_for_secondary_structure as TBD but the routing logic is identical to the roofing cross-sell: don't dismiss, bridge to the specialist who can evaluate.

key_moments.quote: the solar bill that I had was through Sunrun Solar, and the bill was $250 a month. Yet, I still had a $63 electric bill from the electric company. So there lies in the the non trust issue with me
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a prior-bad-experience trust objection, not a vulnerability disclosure. Lisa is citing a specific, named company (Sunrun) and a specific dollar outcome as evidence that the rep's billing claim is unreliable. The rep's response at 05:57 — conceding partial offset is possible — validates her skepticism rather than reframing it. The inversion available here: Sunrun's outcome is evidence of why the design-first approach matters, not evidence that solar doesn't work. The rep had the material to make that move ('that's exactly why we build the design before we make any promises') but didn't.

key_moments.quote: Sometimes we're not able to offset it a 100%. Sometimes we're only able to cover, you know, half of the usage.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the moment the rep's own framing breaks down. Lisa drove the billing education exchange across three turns (03:40, 04:31, 05:18) — the rep was responding to her questions, not volunteering scope-creep content. But at this specific line, the rep crosses from defending the goal to conceding the exception, and does so without the escape hatch. The schema's universal escape hatch ('That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') was available here and would have ended the educational loop while preserving the appointment's credibility.
